metrics.name: BoJ
metrics.question: Will the Bank of Japan raise its short-term policy interest rate at the June 15–16, 2026 Monetary Policy Meeting?

reversal_search.why: every actor already holds a reversal-capable state

simulation_result.question: Will the Bank of Japan raise its short-term policy interest rate at the June 15–16, 2026 Monetary Policy Meeting?
limitations: forecast decomposition — grounded prior 0.875 (counted n=3); simulation-conditional 0.0004 (resolved mass 1.00); headline 0.0043 via combiner_unavailable_range_only. Prior and simulation are reported separately and never blended by a fixed rule.
bounded omitted-state residual 0.631: the interval widens to [0.0, 0.6332] — private-state omissions are BOUNDS, never unknown-state worlds

no leakage-audited prior↔simulation reliability combiner is fitted — the headline is the mass-based recovery blend (resolved mass keeps its simulated conditional; unresolved mass takes the grounded prior); the two inputs are reported separately and never blended by a fixed rule

blueprint.causal_thesis: The BOJ Policy Board votes on a proposal to raise the uncollateralized overnight call rate from 0.75% to 1.0%. A simple majority of the 9 members is required. The outcome hinges on whether the three April dissenters (Nakagawa, Takata, Tamura) are joined by at least two more members, or whether Gover

terminal_canonicalization.why: vote terminals read the tally directly

boj_gradual_hiking_cycle.claim: The Bank of Japan is in a gradual tightening cycle, raising rates in small increments after ending negative rates in March 2024.

provenance.definition: The Bank of Japan is in a gradual tightening cycle, raising rates in small increments after ending negative rates in March 2024.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: BOJ raised policy rate from -0.1% to 0-0.1% in March 2024, then to 0.25% in July 2024, then to 0.5% in January 2025, then to 0.75% in March 2025.
cases.basis_quote: The Bank of Japan decided to raise the guideline for the uncollateralized overnight call rate to approximately 0.75%.

dissenting_pressure_for_hike.claim: Multiple board members have consistently dissented in favor of a rate hike, indicating internal pressure for tightening.

provenance.definition: Multiple board members have consistently dissented in favor of a rate hike, indicating internal pressure for tightening.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: At the April 27-28, 2026 meeting, three members proposed raising the rate to 1.0%, but were defeated 6-3.
cases.basis_quote: Three dissenting members — Nakagawa Junko, Takata Hajime, and Tamura Naoki — proposed raising the rate to 1.0%, but their proposals were defeated.

market_expectations_elevated.claim: Market participants and economists widely expect a rate hike at the June meeting, with Polymarket probability at 63%.

provenance.definition: Market participants and economists widely expect a rate hike at the June meeting, with Polymarket probability at 63%.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: Reuters reported that the BOJ has 'locked in' signals for a June rate hike, and nearly two-thirds of economists expect 1.0% by end-June.
cases.basis_quote: The Bank of Japan has locked in signals for a June rate hike, with nearly two-thirds of economists expecting the rate to reach 1.0% by end-June.

nakagawa_junko.quantity: this member dissents on a rate hold

provenance.definition: Nakagawa Junko votes against maintaining the current rate and proposes a higher rate.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: Any vote where Nakagawa Junko dissents from a decision to keep the rate unchanged.
provenance.exclusion_criteria: Votes where she supports a hold or is absent.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: At the April 27-28, 2026 meeting, Nakagawa Junko proposed raising the rate to 1.0% and dissented from the hold decision.
cases.basis_quote: Three dissenting members — Nakagawa Junko, Takata Hajime, and Tamura Naoki — proposed raising the rate to 1.0%, but their proposals were defeated.

takata_hajime.quantity: this member dissents on a rate hold

provenance.inclusion_criteria: Any vote where Takata Hajime dissents from a decision to keep the rate unchanged.
provenance.exclusion_criteria: Votes where he supports a hold or is absent.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: At the April 27-28, 2026 meeting, Takata Hajime proposed raising the rate to 1.0% and dissented from the hold decision.
cases.basis_quote: Three dissenting members — Nakagawa Junko, Takata Hajime, and Tamura Naoki — proposed raising the rate to 1.0%, but their proposals were defeated.

provenance.definition: Tamura Naoki votes against maintaining the current rate and proposes a higher rate.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: Any vote where Tamura Naoki dissents from a decision to keep the rate unchanged.
provenance.exclusion_criteria: Votes where he supports a hold or is absent.

cases.description: At the April 27-28, 2026 meeting, Tamura Naoki proposed raising the rate to 1.0% and dissented from the hold decision.
cases.basis_quote: Three dissenting members — Nakagawa Junko, Takata Hajime, and Tamura Naoki — proposed raising the rate to 1.0%, but their proposals were defeated.

ueda_kazuo.quantity: this governor votes with the majority on rate decisions

provenance.definition: Governor Ueda votes in favor of the final decision, whether to hold or raise.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: Any vote where Ueda supports the majority decision.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 1 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: At the April 27-28, 2026 meeting, Governor Ueda voted with the majority to keep the rate unchanged at 0.75%.
cases.basis_quote: The Policy Board voted 6-3 to keep the rate unchanged.

outcome_reference_class.quantity: BOJ raises short-term policy interest rate at June 15-16, 2026 meeting

provenance.definition: The BOJ announces a guideline for the uncollateralized overnight call rate higher than the current 0.75%.
provenance.inclusion_criteria: Any increase in the rate, e.g., to 1.0% or higher.
provenance.level_fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 3 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

cases.description: At the March 18-19, 2025 meeting, the BOJ raised the rate from 0.5% to 0.75%.
cases.basis_quote: The Bank of Japan decided to raise the guideline for the uncollateralized overnight call rate to approximately 0.75%.

cases.description: At the January 23-24, 2025 meeting, the BOJ raised the rate from 0.25% to 0.5%.
cases.basis_quote: The Bank of Japan decided to raise the guideline for the uncollateralized overnight call rate to approximately 0.5%.

cases.description: At the July 30-31, 2024 meeting, the BOJ raised the rate from 0-0.1% to 0.25%.
cases.basis_quote: The Bank of Japan decided to raise the guideline for the uncollateralized overnight call rate to approximately 0.25%.

boj_policy_board.consequence_of_nonparticipation: Non-participating members do not affect the vote count; quorum must be met.

weight_invariant.rule: every weight traces to a counted reference class or is explicit unknown mass; no qualitative label is mapped to a number

reversal_search.why: every actor already holds a reversal-capable state

other_members.residual_provenance: uncovered feasible option(s) ['maintain at 0.75%', 'raise to 1.0%'] with no counted cases — bounded per option

ueda_kazuo.claim: Ueda privately believes that the available data by mid-June will be insufficient to confirm that wage-driven inflation is sustainable, and that a premature hike could destabilize markets or the yen carry trade unwinding.
beliefs: Inflation expectations are not yet anchored at 2%
Real wages need more time to turn positive consistently
Global economic uncertainty (e.g., US Fed stance, China slowdown) warrants caution
goals: Maintain credibility of gradual normalization
Avoid triggering a sharp yen appreciation or bond selloff
Preserve board unity
commitments: Data-dependent approach as repeatedly stated in press conferences
ueda_kazuo.pressures: Market expectations for a hike are elevated, but internal dissent from three members is manageable; external pressure from government officials to avoid disrupting growth.
stances: Cautious
Gradualist
supporting_evidence_ids: Oxford Economics' Nagai argument on insufficient data
contradicting_evidence_ids: Reuters 'locked in' signals for June hike
historical_case_refs: BOJ July 2024 hike that was followed by market turmoil and a rapid unwinding
Ueda's cautious stance in early 2024 before April hike
distinguishing_observations: Ueda has not explicitly pre-committed to June action
He emphasized 'patience' in recent speeches
ueda_kazuo.action_if_state: Vote to hold the rate at 0.75%, citing need for more data on wages and consumption.
assumptions: Incoming data (GDP, CPI, tankan) will be mixed

ueda_kazuo.claim: Kazuo Ueda privately believes that the recent dissenting hawkish pressure is based on a misreading of the inflation outlook, and that raising rates now would risk a severe overshoot of the BOJ's inflation target on the downside, derailing the fragile recovery. He sees the 0.75% level as already rest
beliefs: The current 0.75% rate is already above the neutral rate for Japan's economy.
The dissenting members are overreacting to temporary cost-push factors.
Waiting for more data on wage growth and consumption is essential.
goals: Avoid a policy mistake that could tip Japan back into deflation.
Maintain credibility as a cautious, data-dependent governor.
Preserve the BOJ's hard-won credibility after years of ultra-loose policy.
ueda_kazuo.pressures: Intense pressure from three hawkish dissenters and market expectations for a hike, but Ueda feels the institutional majority is with him and that the real economy cannot withstand tighter policy.
stances: Strongly opposed to any rate hike at this meeting.
Favors maintaining the current rate and signaling patience.
supporting_evidence_ids: BOJ's April 2026 statement shows 6-3 vote to hold, with Ueda in the majority.
Japan's core CPI has been trending down toward 2% in recent months.
contradicting_evidence_ids: Three dissenting members proposed a hike to 1.0% in April.
Market expectations are elevated for a hike above 0.75%.
historical_case_refs: 1990-1991: BOJ raised rates too late and then too fast, contributing to asset bubble burst.
2000: BOJ ended zero rates prematurely, then had to reverse.
distinguishing_observations: Ueda has repeatedly emphasized 'data-dependent' approach in past speeches.
He has not signaled any urgency to hike in recent public comments.
ueda_kazuo.action_if_state: Ueda will vote to keep the rate at 0.75% and use his influence as governor to ensure the statement emphasizes uncertainty and the need to wait for more data, overriding the hawkish dissent.

ueda_kazuo.claim: Kazuo Ueda privately believes that while he personally prefers to hold, the persistent and growing dissent from three hawkish members, combined with market expectations, is creating a credibility problem for the BOJ. He fears that if he does not deliver a small hike now, the market will force a larg
beliefs: The cost of not hiking now is greater than the cost of a small hike.
Market expectations are so elevated that a hold would trigger yen depreciation and imported inflation.
A small hike can be reversed later if needed, but a credibility loss cannot.
goals: Prevent a disorderly market reaction and yen collapse.
Maintain BOJ's credibility and his own authority as governor.
Keep the hiking cycle gradual and data-dependent in name, even if not in substance.
ueda_kazuo.pressures: Overwhelming pressure from market expectations, hawkish dissent, and the risk of yen depreciation; institutional majority is fragile and may shift if Ueda signals willingness to compromise.
stances: Reluctantly supports a small hike to 0.875% or 1.0%.
Frames the decision as a 'preemptive adjustment' not a change in stance.
supporting_evidence_ids: Three dissenting members proposed 1.0% in April, showing strong internal pressure.
Market expectations are elevated for a hike above 0.75%.
contradicting_evidence_ids: Ueda's own past statements emphasize caution and data-dependence.
The April vote was 6-3 to hold, indicating majority support for status quo.
historical_case_refs: 2022: BOJ was forced to widen yield band after market pressure built up.
1990s: BOJ's slow response to asset inflation led to a lost decade.
distinguishing_observations: Ueda has not explicitly ruled out a hike in recent weeks.
He has acknowledged that yen depreciation could affect inflation expectations.
ueda_kazuo.action_if_state: Ueda will vote for a small hike (e.g., to 0.875% or 1.0%) and craft the statement to emphasize that this is a 'measured adjustment' consistent with the gradual normalization path, not a shift to aggre

ueda_kazuo.claim: Kazuo Ueda privately believes that the global economic outlook has deteriorated sharply since the April meeting, with new data showing a slowdown in the US and Europe, and that raising rates now would be catastrophic. He sees the dissenting hawks as ignoring international risks and is determined to
beliefs: The global economy is entering a synchronized slowdown that will reduce Japanese export demand.
Raising rates now would be a policy error that could trigger a recession.
The dissenting members are too focused on domestic inflation and ignoring external risks.
goals: Protect the Japanese economy from global headwinds.
Avoid a policy mistake that could be blamed on the BOJ.
Maintain the option to ease policy if the global situation worsens.
ueda_kazuo.pressures: Pressure from hawkish dissenters is countered by new global data; institutional majority may be more willing to hold if Ueda presents a compelling global risk narrative.
stances: Strongly opposed to any rate hike at this meeting.
Favors a clear signal that the BOJ will wait for global conditions to improve.
supporting_evidence_ids: Recent US and European PMI data have shown contraction.
Global trade volumes have declined in May 2026.
contradicting_evidence_ids: Three dissenting members proposed a hike in April and are likely to do so again.
Market expectations are elevated for a hike.
historical_case_refs: 2008: BOJ held rates steady during the global financial crisis, then cut.
2011: BOJ eased after the earthquake, showing willingness to respond to external shocks.
distinguishing_observations: Ueda has recently mentioned 'uncertainty in the global economy' in a speech.
He has access to confidential BOJ staff reports on global conditions.
ueda_kazuo.action_if_state: Ueda will vote to hold at 0.75% and use the statement to highlight global risks, effectively arguing that the BOJ must remain accommodative to support the economy through external headwinds.

nakagawa_junko.claim: Nakagawa is convinced that inflation is sustainably above target and that delaying a hike risks falling behind the curve, damaging BOJ credibility.
beliefs: Core CPI will remain above 2% through 2026
Wage negotiations in spring 2026 yielded strong increases
The yen's weakness is importing inflation that requires a rate response
goals: Raise rate to 1.0% to preempt overheating
Signal BOJ commitment to normalization
Reduce risk of yen depreciation
nakagawa_junko.pressures: Market expectations for a hike support her position; internal pressure from Governor to wait; external pressure from export industries to keep yen weak.
stances: Hawkish
Preemptive
supporting_evidence_ids: April 28 dissenting proposal to raise to 1.0%
historical_case_refs: BOJ's 2000-2006 tightening cycle where dissenters were later vindicated
Similar hawkish dissent by member Kataoka in 2018
distinguishing_observations: She has been consistently hawkish since appointment
Her proposals have gained support from two other members
nakagawa_junko.action_if_state: Propose a 25bp hike to 1.0% and vote in favor, regardless of outcome.
assumptions: Incoming data will support her view
Other two dissenters will join her

nakagawa_junko.claim: Nakagawa Junko privately believes that global financial conditions are deteriorating rapidly, with a high probability of a systemic shock (e.g., a US recession or European debt crisis) that would make a rate hike dangerously pro-cyclical. She views the April 2026 dissenting vote as a tactical signal
beliefs: Global economic slowdown is accelerating
A rate hike in June would amplify financial instability
The BOJ must prioritize financial stability over inflation targeting
goals: Avoid a policy mistake that could trigger a credit crunch
Preserve BOJ credibility as a stabilizer
nakagawa_junko.pressures: Market expectations for a hike are overblown; dissenting pressure from allies is based on outdated data
stances: Cautious on further tightening
Favor maintaining current rate or even a pause
historical_case_refs: BOJ 2000 rate hike reversal
ECB 2011 rate hike reversal
distinguishing_observations: Nakagawa has privately expressed concern about global bond market volatility
She has recently met with financial stability officials
nakagawa_junko.action_if_state: Vote to keep the rate at 0.75% or propose a symbolic 'wait-and-see' language, breaking with Takata and Tamura.

nakagawa_junko.claim: Nakagawa Junko privately believes that Japan's core inflation is about to overshoot the 2% target significantly due to a delayed pass-through of import costs and a tight labor market. She views the April 2026 dissenting vote as a genuine first step, and now sees 1.0% as insufficient—she wants 1.25%
beliefs: Inflation expectations are becoming unanchored
The BOJ's own forecasts are too optimistic
A larger hike is needed to maintain credibility
goals: Front-load tightening to prevent overheating
Signal independence from dovish board members
nakagawa_junko.pressures: Dissenting allies may be too cautious; market expectations are still too low
stances: Aggressively hawkish
Favor a hike above 1.0%
historical_case_refs: BOJ 1989-1990 tightening cycle
Fed 2022 front-loading
distinguishing_observations: Nakagawa has cited recent Tokyo CPI data above 3% in private meetings
She has argued that the BOJ's 'patience' is a mistake
nakagawa_junko.action_if_state: Propose a hike to 1.25% or higher, and if defeated, issue a stronger dissenting statement calling for more aggressive action.

nakagawa_junko.claim: Nakagawa Junko privately believes that the BOJ must raise rates to 1.0% to align with global peers and maintain yen stability, but she is willing to accept a compromise of 0.75% with a clear forward guidance for a July hike. She views the April 2026 dissenting vote as a negotiating tactic, and her p
beliefs: A divided board harms policy transmission
Yen depreciation is a key risk
Gradualism is still viable if credible
goals: Achieve a rate hike by July at the latest
Maintain board unity and public confidence
nakagawa_junko.pressures: Ueda's opposition is strong; market expectations for a hike are high but fragile
stances: Pragmatic hawkish
Favor a hike but open to sequencing
historical_case_refs: BOJ 2006-2007 gradual normalization
Fed 2015-2016 liftoff
distinguishing_observations: Nakagawa has privately signaled willingness to 'meet halfway'
She has discussed a 'conditional commitment' with allies
nakagawa_junko.action_if_state: Vote for a hold at 0.75% but with explicit language that a hike is likely in July, and privately urge Takata and Tamura to support this compromise.

takata_hajime.claim: Takata is open to a hike in June but only if specific conditions are met, such as clear evidence of wage pass-through to services prices and stable global financial conditions.
beliefs: Inflation is driven by demand, not supply shocks
BOJ should not be bound by calendar but by data
A hike in June is possible but not inevitable
goals: Normalize policy at a measured pace
Avoid being seen as a permanent dissenter
Maintain influence on future decisions
takata_hajime.pressures: Market expectations for a hike; internal pressure from Governor to align; external pressure from financial markets for clarity.
stances: Hawkish but pragmatic
Data-dependent
historical_case_refs: BOJ member Sakurai's conditional hawkish stance in 2024
Fed's Waller's data-dependent pivot
distinguishing_observations: He has not explicitly committed to June
His language in speeches has been more nuanced than Nakagawa's
takata_hajime.action_if_state: Vote for a hike if data supports; otherwise, vote to hold and explain conditions not met.
assumptions: May CPI and wage data will be available before meeting

takata_hajime.claim: Takata believes that the current 0.75% rate is dangerously accommodative given that core inflation is persistently above 2% and wage-price spiral risks are materializing, requiring an immediate hike to 1.0% to preempt an overshoot.
beliefs: Core CPI will exceed 2.5% by Q3 2026 if rates stay at 0.75%
The BOJ's own forecasts underestimate demand-driven inflation
Waiting until July risks losing credibility and forcing a larger hike later
goals: Anchor inflation expectations firmly at 2%
Prevent yen depreciation from fueling import price pass-through
Demonstrate independence from political pressure to keep rates low
takata_hajime.pressures: Strong market expectations for a hike; dissenting minority at April meeting; yen at 155 vs USD
stances: Vote for 1.0% at June meeting
Argue that gradual hiking is insufficient given data momentum
Dismiss concerns about growth impact as overblown
supporting_evidence_ids: April 2026 CPI release showing core at 2.3%
BOJ April Outlook Report upward revision for FY2026 inflation
Market pricing of 80% probability of hike by July
contradicting_evidence_ids: BOJ April statement noting consumption weakness
Global bond yields declining on recession fears
historical_case_refs: BOJ's 2000 rate hike reversal after premature tightening
Fed's 2021-2022 delayed response to inflation
distinguishing_observations: Takata's public speeches emphasize 'vigilance' on inflation
He voted for 1.0% in April despite majority opposition
takata_hajime.action_if_state: Takata will forcefully advocate for a 1.0% rate at the June meeting, propose an explicit statement that further hikes are data-dependent but likely, and threaten to dissent publicly if the majority ho

takata_hajime.claim: Takata is privately convinced that the biggest risk is not inflation but financial instability from prolonged low rates—specifically, zombie firms and bank margin compression—and that a hike to 1.0% is needed to force restructuring and normalize monetary policy before the next shock.
beliefs: Low rates are keeping unproductive firms alive, suppressing productivity growth
Regional banks' net interest margins are unsustainably thin at 0.75%
A gradual hike now reduces the risk of a sudden crash later
goals: Promote creative destruction in the corporate sector
Strengthen bank profitability to ensure lending capacity
Normalize policy to regain ammunition for future downturns
takata_hajime.pressures: BOJ Financial System Report flags rising credit risk; IMF Article IV consultation recommends gradual normalization; LDP lawmakers concerned about bank health
stances: Vote for 1.0% as a structural reform measure
Downplay near-term inflation concerns as transitory
Emphasize macroprudential benefits of higher rates
supporting_evidence_ids: BOJ April Financial System Report noting increased corporate debt at risk
Declining number of listed firms and rising M&A activity
Bank of Japan staff estimates of zombie firm share at 15%
contradicting_evidence_ids: GDP growth below potential in Q1 2026
Government bond market functioning well at current rates
historical_case_refs: Japan's 'lost decade' exacerbated by forbearance lending
ECB's 2011 rate hike to address financial stability, later reversed
distinguishing_observations: Takata's background includes research on corporate governance
He has privately expressed concern about 'zombie firms' to staff
takata_hajime.action_if_state: Takata will vote for 1.0% but frame his dissent around financial stability rather than inflation, and may propose a conditional commitment to hold rates if financial conditions tighten excessively.

takata_hajime.claim: Takata believes that the BOJ must hike to 1.0% to align with global central banks (Fed, ECB) and prevent yen depreciation from becoming disorderly, which would destabilize Asian financial markets and force an emergency response later.
beliefs: The Fed will hold rates above 5% through 2026, keeping USD strong
Yen at 160 would trigger capital outflows from Japan and regional contagion
BOJ independence is undermined if it does not respond to FX moves
goals: Stabilize yen around 140-145 to reduce import cost volatility
Maintain policy credibility vis-à-vis G7 peers
Avoid a repeat of the 2024 yen crisis that required MOF intervention
takata_hajime.pressures: Yen at 155 and weakening; US Treasury yields elevated; Asian central banks (Korea, Indonesia) raising rates preemptively
stances: Vote for 1.0% citing external balance risks
Argue that domestic conditions are secondary to global spillovers
Support coordinated communication with other central banks
supporting_evidence_ids: BOJ April meeting minutes noting 'close attention to FX'
IMF warning on spillovers from asynchronous normalization
contradicting_evidence_ids: Japan's current account surplus remains large
Inflation expectations in Japan are still below 2% in long-term surveys
historical_case_refs: BOJ's 2022-2024 yield curve control exit driven by yen weakness
Swiss National Bank's 2015 floor removal and subsequent FX intervention
distinguishing_observations: Takata has met with MOF officials on FX matters
He referenced 'global monetary conditions' in a recent speech
takata_hajime.action_if_state: Takata will vote for 1.0% and push for a statement that explicitly mentions 'external economic and financial developments' as a factor, and may propose a joint G7 statement on exchange rate stability.

tamura_naoki.claim: Tamura believes that the BOJ's ultra-loose policy has created distortions that must be corrected urgently, and that a hike in June is necessary to prevent financial imbalances.
beliefs: Negative real rates are fueling asset bubbles
The BOJ's credibility is at stake if it delays
Inflation expectations have become unanchored on the upside
goals: Raise rates to neutral as soon as possible
Prevent a future crash
Restore BOJ independence from political pressure
tamura_naoki.pressures: Market expectations for a hike; internal pressure from Governor; external pressure from academics and media criticizing slow normalization.
stances: Hawkish
Structural reformer
historical_case_refs: BOJ member Iwata's persistent hawkish dissent in 2013-2014
Similar structural hawkishness by ECB's Weidmann
distinguishing_observations: He has been the most consistent hawk on the board
His views are well-known and priced in
tamura_naoki.action_if_state: Propose a hike to 1.0% and vote in favor, possibly with a stronger statement urging faster normalization.
transition_triggers: Sharp rise in stock market or real estate prices

tamura_naoki.claim: Tamura Naoki privately believes that the global economic outlook has deteriorated sharply since the April meeting, with new data showing a synchronized slowdown in major economies and heightened financial instability. He views further rate hikes as dangerously premature and fears triggering a recess
beliefs: Recent global PMI and trade data indicate a synchronized downturn
Financial stress indicators (credit spreads, VIX) have spiked
Raising rates now would amplify external shocks and risk deflation
goals: Avoid a policy mistake that could destabilize the economy
Preserve BOJ credibility by not acting against deteriorating conditions
tamura_naoki.pressures: Majority of board favors hold; market expectations for a hike are elevated but could reverse on bad news; dissenting allies Nakagawa and Takata may still push for a hike, creating internal tension.
stances: Vote to hold the rate at 0.75% or lower
Advocate for a cautious, data-dependent pause
historical_case_refs: BOJ 2008 pause after Lehman shock
Fed 2019 mid-cycle cut reversal
distinguishing_observations: Tamura's public statements shift from hawkish to cautious
He cites global risks in internal discussions
tamura_naoki.action_if_state: Tamura votes to keep the uncollateralized overnight call rate at 0.75% or lower, joining the majority and potentially flipping the outcome if his vote is decisive.

tamura_naoki.claim: Tamura Naoki privately prioritizes institutional cohesion and the BOJ's credibility above his personal hawkish views. He believes that a divided board and a surprise hike would undermine the BOJ's communication strategy and policy predictability, so he chooses to support the majority's decision to h
beliefs: A 6-3 vote with a dissenting minority is acceptable but a 5-4 split would signal disarray
The BOJ's forward guidance and gradual approach must be maintained
His role is to ensure smooth policy implementation, not to force a rate change
goals: Maintain BOJ's reputation for stability and consensus
Avoid market turmoil from a surprise decision
tamura_naoki.pressures: Majority clearly favors hold; dissenting allies may pressure him to join them, but institutional norms and Governor Ueda's influence are strong.
stances: Vote with the majority to hold
Publicly emphasize the need for unity and data dependence
historical_case_refs: BOJ 2000 rate hike reversal under institutional pressure
Fed 2015-2016 gradualist approach
distinguishing_observations: Tamura votes with the majority despite prior dissent
He issues a statement supporting the hold decision
tamura_naoki.action_if_state: Tamura votes to hold the rate at 0.75%, aligning with the majority and ensuring a 7-2 or 6-3 outcome that maintains the status quo.

tamura_naoki.claim: Tamura Naoki privately believes that the April hold was a mistake and that inflation expectations are becoming unanchored. He sees new data showing wage-price spiral risks and yen depreciation accelerating, and he is now convinced that a larger hike to 1.25% is necessary to preempt overheating, maki
beliefs: Core inflation is accelerating above 3% and broadening
Wage negotiations have yielded 5%+ increases, feeding demand
Yen weakness is importing inflation and must be countered decisively
goals: Crush inflation expectations before they become entrenched
Restore BOJ credibility as an inflation fighter
tamura_naoki.pressures: Majority favors hold; dissenting allies Nakagawa and Takata may support a smaller hike, creating a split among hawks; market expectations for a hike are elevated but not for such a large move.
stances: Propose a hike to 1.25% or higher
Argue for a faster normalization pace
historical_case_refs: BOJ 1990-1991 tightening cycle
Volcker 1979-1980 aggressive rate hikes
distinguishing_observations: Tamura proposes a larger hike than his previous 1.0% proposal
He cites new inflation and wage data in his dissent
tamura_naoki.action_if_state: Tamura votes for a rate hike to 1.25% or higher, dissenting from the majority and potentially rallying other hawks, but likely still losing the vote.

other_members.claim: The remaining three members (excluding Ueda and the three dissenters) are aligned with Ueda's cautious approach and will vote to hold, believing that more time is needed to assess the economic outlook.
beliefs: Inflation is moderating
Wage growth is not yet broad-based
Global risks justify patience
goals: Maintain policy stability
Avoid rocking markets
Support Governor's leadership
commitments: Public statements echoing Ueda's data dependence
other_members.pressures: Market expectations for a hike; internal pressure from dissenters; external pressure from government for stability.
stances: Cautious
Status quo
historical_case_refs: BOJ board dynamics in 2024 where majority held despite dissent
Fed's FOMC majority cohesion under Powell
distinguishing_observations: They have not publicly indicated a change in stance
Their votes are predictable based on past behavior
other_members.action_if_state: Vote to hold the rate at 0.75%, maintaining the status quo.
assumptions: No dramatic change in economic data

other_members.claim: The other members believe that raising the rate above 0.75% now would risk overshooting the neutral rate, given the gradual hiking cycle and elevated market expectations, potentially triggering a recession or financial instability.
beliefs: The neutral rate is close to 0.75% and further hikes would be contractionary.
Market expectations are already elevated and a hike could cause a sharp tightening of financial conditions.
The gradual hiking cycle should pause to assess the cumulative impact of past hikes.
goals: Avoid an economic downturn.
Maintain financial stability.
Preserve the credibility of the gradual hiking strategy.
other_members.pressures: Institutional majority and desire for consensus, but dissenting pressure for hike creates tension.
stances: Vote to keep the rate at 0.75%.
Argue for a wait-and-see approach in the statement.
historical_case_refs: BOJ's 2000-2006 experience with premature tightening after deflation.
ECB's 2011 rate hike followed by reversal.
distinguishing_observations: The April vote was 6-3 to hold, indicating majority caution.
No clear evidence of inflation accelerating above target.
other_members.action_if_state: Vote to maintain the rate at 0.75% and craft a statement emphasizing patience and data-dependence.

other_members.claim: The other members believe that the three dissenting votes for 1.0% in April signal a strong internal and external consensus for a hike, and that failing to raise now would damage the BOJ's credibility and allow inflation expectations to become unanchored.
beliefs: The dissenting votes reflect a broader market and political pressure for normalization.
Waiting risks falling behind the curve, requiring larger hikes later.
The economy can withstand a gradual increase to 1.0% given current conditions.
goals: Maintain credibility with markets and the government.
Prevent inflation expectations from drifting above target.
Align with the global tightening cycle.
other_members.pressures: Institutional majority but now leaning toward action due to dissenting pressure and market expectations.
stances: Vote to raise the rate to 1.0%.
Support a hawkish statement signaling further hikes.
historical_case_refs: BOJ's 2024 rate hike from negative territory to 0.25%.
Federal Reserve's 2022 preemptive tightening.
distinguishing_observations: Three members already voted for 1.0% in April, showing a faction for action.
Market pricing may already incorporate a hike.
other_members.action_if_state: Vote to raise the rate to 1.0% and issue a statement with a hawkish forward guidance.

other_members.claim: The other members are deeply divided, with some fearing overshoot and others pushing for a hike, leading to a compromise where the rate is raised to 0.875% (a symbolic increase above 0.75%) to satisfy both camps and avoid a repeat of the 6-3 split.
beliefs: A small increase above 0.75% is a middle ground that signals progress without shocking markets.
The exact level matters less than the direction of policy.
Maintaining unity on the board is crucial for policy effectiveness.
goals: Achieve consensus on the board.
Signal a continued normalization path.
Avoid a public split that could undermine credibility.
other_members.pressures: Institutional majority but internal disagreement; need to balance dissenting pressure with caution.
stances: Vote for a rate of 0.875% (or a range above 0.75%).
Craft a statement that emphasizes gradualism and data-dependence.
historical_case_refs: BOJ's 2023 decision to widen the yield band as a compromise.
Federal Reserve's 2015-2016 gradual rate path with internal dissent.
distinguishing_observations: The April vote was 6-3, indicating a clear majority but significant minority.
A compromise rate could be a novel solution to bridge the gap.
other_members.action_if_state: Vote for a rate of 0.875% and issue a statement highlighting the board's unity and gradual approach.

boj_policy_board.consequence_of_nonparticipation: Non-participating members do not affect the vote count; quorum must be met.

checks.note: all terminal-writing effects are known mechanical kinds
at least one resolving path exists
vote tally in members — exact units
synthetic YES→1 / NO→0 through the live path

deliberations.trigger: internal_conflict_named: Whether the recent data sufficiently confirms that demand pressures are sustained enough to justify a hike now, or if waiting would be
deliberations.note: I've weighed the tradeoff: hiking now aligns with my hawkish stance and avoids being sidelined, but recent data on demand pressures is mixed—consumption is steady but not accelerating, and wage growth

deliberations.trigger: internal_conflict_named: Whether Nakagawa and Takata will support a smaller hike or join me in a larger move.
deliberations.note: I am torn between my conviction that a decisive hike to 1.25% is needed to crush inflation and yen weakness, and the reality that the majority favors a hold. My hawkish allies Nakagawa and Takata may

deliberations.trigger: internal_conflict_named: Whether the recent wage data and inflation prints are sufficient to justify a hike now, or if waiting would risk falling behind the cur
deliberations.note: The recent wage data shows some improvement but not yet broad-based, and inflation expectations remain below 2%. A hike now could trigger yen appreciation and bond selloff, undermining gradual normali

deliberations.trigger: internal_conflict_named: Whether the dissenters' arguments have gained any last-minute traction among undecided members.
deliberations.note: The dissenters' arguments for a hike are based on inflation persistence and wage growth, but my private beliefs indicate inflation is moderating and wage growth is not yet broad-based. Global risks ju

deliberations.trigger: internal_conflict_named: Whether Nakagawa and Takata will actually dissent and push for a hike, creating internal tension.
deliberations.note: The core tension is between my conviction to hold rates and the possibility that Nakagawa and Takata will push for a hike, creating a public split that could undermine the board's credibility. However

deliberations.trigger: internal_conflict_named: Whether a gradual approach with a clear signal for July would be seen as credible or as weakness.
deliberations.first_action: Propose a compromise: maintain now but commit to a July hike with a clear forward guidance
deliberations.final_action: Propose a compromise: maintain rate at 0.75% now but commit to a July hike with strong, data-contingent forward guidance
deliberations.note: The compromise of maintaining now with a July commitment risks being perceived as weakness, especially given Ueda's strong opposition and fragile market expectations. However, a divided board would ha

deliberations.trigger: internal_conflict_named: Whether the recent cost-push pressures will translate into sustained wage growth and demand-driven inflation.
deliberations.note: The hawks are pushing for a hike based on cost-push inflation, but Japan's history shows that tightening prematurely against supply shocks can crush fragile demand. My own analysis indicates the neutr

deliberations.trigger: internal_conflict_named: Whether the recent inflation is truly demand-driven or will prove transitory.
deliberations.note: I've weighed the tradeoff: hiking now could signal confidence and align with market expectations, but the data on demand-driven inflation remains inconclusive. Governor Ueda pressures for alignment, b

deliberations.trigger: internal_conflict_named: Whether the recent economic data would have justified a hike if we had more time to analyze it.
deliberations.note: I have weighed the tradeoff: a hike now would signal confidence but risks disrupting fragile real wage growth and triggering yen appreciation or bond selloff, while waiting preserves credibility and b

deliberations.trigger: internal_conflict_named: Whether a majority of other members will support a hold with forward guidance or demand an immediate hike.
deliberations.final_action: Maintain the compromise proposal: hold at 0.75% now with a firm commitment to a July hike backed by data-contingent forw
deliberations.note: The board is deeply divided. An immediate hike risks a 3-3 split, damaging unity and policy transmission. My compromise of a hold with a strong July commitment is the only path to maintain credibility

coalescer.randomness_rule: content-addressed (state/context-seeded) draws — equal keys share the future transition law by construction; independent streams carry a tag that refuses the merge

engine_primary.weight_source: counted_reference_class_posteriors (no qualitative label is mapped to a number anywhere in this engine); represented states carry the full branch mass — omitted-state residuals are bounded interval-wideners, never unknown-state worlds

skipped_reasons: no deterministic trigger fired: primary stable, no verified evidence conflict, no reversal-capable disputed assumption (imagination alone is not a trigger)

replicate_policy.reason: behavioral replicates not requested (deterministic execution: content-addressed draws make replicate 0 exhaustive)

treatments.unresolved_truncation: preserved as unfinished branch mass; disclosed

provenance.quantity: BOJ raises short-term policy interest rate at June 15-16, 2026 meeting
provenance.fallback_reason: all levels sparse (total 3 usable case(s)); rate is wide by construction

notes: no leakage-audited reliability combiner is fitted — prior and simulation are reported separately with the feasible combined range; no fixed blend is applied and Lean V2 must not become default on this basis

consequences.boundary_contract: delivery mechanics precompiled; interpretation/persuasion/reaction remain branch-local actor decisions

vote_on_rate.description: Each Policy Board member casts vote on proposed rate.

novel_propose_compromise_rate_guidance.description: Nakagawa Junko proposes a compromise to maintain the rate at 0.75% now but commit to a July hike with strong, data-contingent forward guidance.

params.value: Proposal: maintain rate at 0.75% now, commit to July hike with data-contingent forward guidance
